profile.name: Calvin Hou
profile.title: Electrical Engineering major
profile.scopeBrief: EE @ Georgia Tech · robotics · PCBs · embedded control. Pick a channel below.

about.paragraph: I'm an Electrical Engineering student with a strong background in building reliable robotics + hardware systems. My passion mostly lies in building self-sufficient autonomous systems. My technical background consists mostly of various hardware projects, designing, building, debugging, and validating.

My work background includes the validation and assembly of cutting-edge AI server hardware, robot design and development, and electrical system design for various on-campus competition teams, such as our FSAE and Rocketry Teams.

experience.title: Avionics Engineer
experience.description:
* Responsible Engineer on the Control Test Vehicle, leading design and integration of an STM32-based PCB for controlling various actuators and fans.
* Leading Ethernet implementation on board, optimizing packaging and signal integrity for noisy EMI environments
* Designed buck converters & LDOs (22.2V to 12V/4.4V/3.3V) using STM32's integrated ADCs for real-time power rail telemetry.

experience.title: Research and Design Intern
experience.description:
* Engineered and validated a next-generation (V2) liquid cooling system by leading testing and integration efforts on the Liquid Pump Unit (LPU), and Coolant Distribution Unit (CDU). 
* Conducted rigorous pressure and leak testing to ensure system integrity, achieving a 100% pass rate across validation cycles. 
* Assembled and wired multiple CDU units, integrating distributed leak detection sensors and verifying functionality through controlled fault injection and response testing.
* Designed and implemented temperature monitoring solutions using welded thermistors, collecting and analyzing large datasets to benchmark V2 performance against prior V1 systems.
*  Identified thermal inefficiencies and introduced targeted airflow optimizations—including custom air guides, which improved radiator cooling uniformity and reduced temperature variance across critical components.

experience.title: Advanced Robotics Centre Research Intern
experience.description:
Worked under researcher Marcelo Ang to explore emerging applications in humanoid and soft robotics, contributing to early-stage research on automating cargo lashing operations in high-throughput maritime ports such as Singapore. The project aimed to replace a traditionally manual, labor-intensive process with a scalable robotic solution.

* Developed a functional proof-of-concept system using a custom-built 3DOF robotic arm to simulate automated lashing and unlashing tasks. Self-taught C++ to implement low-level servo control, and engineered a custom hardware controller to interface with the system.
*  Designed and deployed inverse kinematics and velocity control algorithms, improving end-effector positioning accuracy and motion smoothness by an estimated 40% compared to initial direct-control methods.

Demonstrated the feasibility of automating repetitive port operations using cost-effective, widely accessible components, validating that robust performance could be achieved without specialized hardware. This work established a foundation for scaling toward higher-DOF systems and more complex real-world deployment scenarios, supporting broader efforts in industrial robotics automation.

experience.title: Amazon Future Engineer Program Participant
experience.description:
* **Internship still in progress; More work to be done!!**

* Discovered fragmented, product-siloed MCAL implementations — refactored and standardized 6 STM32 modules (PWM, ADC, DAC, CAN FD, UART, Watchdog) into a shared library, eliminating redundant rewrites for 8 engineers across 6 bespoke PSU/supercap designs used in Fortune 10 hyperscale cloud and semiconductor companies.

* Root-caused EMF-induced signal faults in production data center PSUs through board-level debugging, then implemented and validated 21 filter configurations (IIR averaging + configurable glitch filtering) in STM32 embedded C — reducing false fault triggers and exposing tunable parameters as user-configurable endpoints

* Wrote exhaustive Python bench test suites per module — the PWM suite alone validated 900 hardware configurations (9 timers × 4 channels × 5 frequencies × 5 duty cycles), establishing operational min/max bounds and generating evidence used for UL60730-1 certification
